aliases: Samosa with Yoghurt and Mint Sauce

english: Samosa with Yoghurt and Mint Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Samosa Dahi Chaat; mapped to the broader chaat food product class as the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_description: Savoury snack — mixed dish with pastry and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Samosa Dahi Chaat; A0CKP (savoury snack, mixed dish) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific chaat preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Samosa Dahi Chaat is approximately 200 g, comprising two to three sliced samosas topped with yoghurt, mint chutney, tamarind chutney, and chaat masala.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa Dahi Chat — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Samosa Dahi Chat (cr_id G00011076). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Samosa and Chaat-type dishes — analogous estimate based on potato-filled pastry and refined wheat flour snack data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Samosa Dahi Chaat in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the closest analogous foods: deep-fried potato-filled wheat pastry snacks (GI ~70–75) and refined wheat flour-based chaat preparations. The yoghurt component provides minor protein buffering but does not substantially lower the overall GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (70 × 33.16 × 2) / 100 = 46.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 388 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (166 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Samosa Dahi Chaat due to its high sodium content (388 mg/100 g), which is compounded by chaat masala and tamarind chutney toppings; if consumed, request minimal chutney and chaat masala and restrict portion to no more than 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from refined wheat pastry (33.2 g carbohydrate/100 g, GI ~70) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) and added sugars from tamarind chutney
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Samosa Dahi Chaat because the deep-fried refined wheat pastry and potato filling produce a high glycaemic load (~46 GL per serving), and the tamarind chutney adds concentrated sugar; if consumed, request omission of sweet chutneys and limit portion to 100 g or less.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.47 g/100 g) including trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying, elevated sodium (388 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (14 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Samosa Dahi Chaat due to its high fat content from deep-frying, the presence of trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) which is strongly atherogenic, and elevated sodium; if consumed on rare occasions, choose a baked samosa variant and request minimal chutney to reduce sodium and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs; potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables; trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Samosa Dahi Chaat as the deep-fried samosa base generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), the potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables that may trigger joint flares, and trans fat at any amount is strongly pro-inflammatory; a baked samosa variant with yoghurt only and no chutney is a safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa Dahi Chaat is a popular Indian street food and chaat preparation widely served in Singapore's Indian food stalls and hawker centres. It is made by slicing or crushing deep-fried samosas — triangular wheat flour pastry parcels filled with spiced potato and peas — and topping them generously with dahi (plain yoghurt), green mint chutney, sweet tamarind chutney, chaat masala, and freshly chopped coriander leaves. The dish delivers a complex interplay of textures (crispy pastry softened by yoghurt) and flavours (tangy, sweet, spicy, and savoury simultaneously). Per 100 g, it provides 310 kcal, 5.14 g protein, 17.47 g fat (including 0.2 g trans fat from deep-frying), 33.16 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 388 mg sodium. The high carbohydrate content from refined wheat pastry and potato filling, combined with a high estimated glycaemic index (~70), makes this dish a high glycaemic load food. The elevated sodium from chaat masala and chutneys raises concern for hypertension. The trans fat from deep-frying is a cardiovascular risk factor. Yoghurt provides probiotic benefit and minor protein, but is insufficient to offset the overall nutritional concerns. This dish is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and dairy allergens. It is typically consumed as a snack, starter, or light meal at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat samosa dahi chaat?
Is samosa dahi chaat high in sodium?
What is dahi chaat made of?

ingredient_synonyms.samosa: samosa pastry, fried pastry parcel, samoosa
ingredient_synonyms.chaat masala: chaat spice blend, Indian spice mix

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Samosa Dahi Chat (cr_id G00011076) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but is clearly documented. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Samosa Dahi Chaat is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00011076) as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g of 0.2 g/100 g is carried forward from the input health_suitability clinical notes referencing the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for deep-fried potato-filled wheat pastry snacks; no direct GI measurement for Samosa Dahi Chaat was found. Chinese name is a transliteration approximation as no standard Chinese culinary name exists for this dish in Singapore; the transliteration 萨摩萨达希聊天 / 薩摩薩希聊天 is functional for retrieval purposes.
